episode_meta.title: Olle Zacharison: How BBC News is Shaping its AI Strategy for the Next Era of Journalism

ai_tools: Synthetic voice / text-to-speech (for My Club Daily podcast and article read-aloud)

use_cases.description: AI-assisted translation across BBC World Service's 42 languages, with side-by-side interface showing original text, AI-translated version, and human sub-edited final version
use_cases.outcome: Most mature AI project at BBC; precise metrics on time savings vs. manual workflow; boosted output specifically in BBC Mundo (Latin American section)

use_cases.description: Large-scale transcription of audio and video content to make it searchable and enable downstream reformatting workflows
use_cases.outcome: Helps journalists find content more easily and enables content to be reformatted across formats

use_cases.description: Using customized GPTs to assist newsletter production by suggesting subject lines, text fields, and other instrumental elements for BBC's 10 newsletters
use_cases.outcome: Automates tedious text field generation while preserving journalists' creative writing for lead paragraphs and analysis

use_cases.description: AI-generated alt text (image descriptions) for accessibility, replacing fully manual production to support screen reader users
use_cases.outcome: Benchmarked exact time savings down to the second between manual and AI-powered workflows

use_cases.description: AI-powered reformatting of video content across different aspect ratios and formats (e.g., vertical 9:16 for social media), including automated subtitles and design template suggestions
use_cases.outcome: Aims to reduce duplication where the same news video topic is produced in five different places without synchronization

use_cases.description: My Club Daily podcast: converting text-based football articles into short two-minute daily audio podcasts using AI-generated scripts and synthetic voice, covering five Premier League clubs
use_cases.outcome: Launched as a live product; increases content output by repurposing existing articles into new audio format

use_cases.description: Using third-party tools like NotebookLM to help journalists sift through large datasets, chat with spreadsheets, and find story angles in big data for investigative journalism
use_cases.tool_used: Google NotebookLM and other third-party tools
use_cases.outcome: Part of augmenting journalism bucket; gives reporters tools to produce better original investigations

use_cases.description: Copilot deployed across the BBC for general-purpose AI assistance, with increasing month-over-month usage and journalist testimonials of improved workflows
use_cases.outcome: Usage increasing month by month; journalists report improved work in learning lunches

use_cases.description: BBC bespoke fine-tuned LLM ('Style Assist') that rewrites local democracy reporter articles to align with BBC editorial style, tone, and formatting standards before publication
use_cases.outcome: Trained on paired datasets of incoming articles and edited/published versions; performs well on style alignment but challenges remain with political impartiality nuances; aims to publish more of the 60,000 annual submissions (currently only 12,000-15,000 published)

use_cases.description: Synthetic voice read-aloud of text articles as a new user experience innovation to give audiences alternative ways to consume content
use_cases.outcome: Identified as starting point for innovating user experiences

use_cases.description: Conversational news service (chat-like news interface) developed at Swedish Radio as a beta test, offering hyper-personalized news via pre-suggested prompts and free-text queries
use_cases.outcome: Live beta since March; early insights show 80% of users press pre-suggested questions rather than typing their own queries

use_cases.description: Personal use of a customized GPT ('British Lingo Decoder') to explain BBC-specific colloquial terms and acronyms in both English and Swedish
use_cases.outcome: Hardly ever fails; colleagues have asked him to license it out

use_cases.description: Personal use of a customized GPT ('Better English GPT') for translating and refining formal written communications as a non-native English speaker
use_cases.outcome: Used routinely for formal emails to HR and colleagues

key_claims.claim: BBC News is organized around four strategic AI themes: boost productivity, reformat news content, augment journalism, and innovate user experiences
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: BBC News has 5,500 people across the world
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: The BBC operates in 42 languages through its World Service
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: The BBC funds 150 local democracy reporters across the UK who submit 60,000 stories a year, of which only 12,000-15,000 are published due to manual sub-editing requirements
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: All languages work better with AI when English is involved as one of the languages; going between two smaller languages is much more challenging
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: At Swedish Radio, after a basic AI training course for all staff (1,400 people), 1,224 ideas were submitted through a free text field asking what people would use an AI assistant for
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: 80% of users in Swedish conversational news services (Aftonbladet and Swedish Radio) press pre-suggested questions rather than typing their own queries
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: AI return on investment is often negative at this stage because it requires more investment than what you get back; capabilities are still being built up
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: The BBC has the most trusted news brand in the whole world, with millions of people consuming BBC News globally and significant direct traffic to its website and app
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: BBC research has shown very clearly how AI assistants are distorting BBC content with hallucinations and false attributions to the BBC brand
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: The BBC's fine-tuned LLM performs well at aligning with BBC style but struggles with political impartiality nuances, especially undertext in local political reporting
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

key_claims.claim: News consumption in AI assistants is still low; it is not the primary use case, but the BBC is observing this closely
key_claims.speaker: Olle Zacharison

challenges.description: AI quality varies significantly between languages; translation between two smaller (non-English) languages is much more challenging than when English is involved
Journalists are in near-100% production mode and lack time to explore and experiment with AI tools, leading to low adoption not from skepticism but from lack of bandwidth
Centralized AI initiatives are easier to track and showcase, but the more invisible personal adoption by individual journalists may be even more impactful and harder to cultivate
Organizational silos in large media companies: editorial, product, R&D, and technology teams sit far apart at the base of the organizational pyramid and often do not speak the same cultural language
Media companies historically launch MVP products and jump to the next project without iterating, failing to follow through on user journey refinement
The fine-tuned BBC LLM struggles with political impartiality nuances, particularly capturing the undertext of local political arguments and giving all sides fair representation
AI assistants and aggregators are distorting and hallucinating BBC content with false attributions to the BBC brand, threatening editorial integrity
ROI for AI projects is currently negative; precise monetary measurement is difficult and most projects require more investment than they return at this stage
Moving AI pilots from editorial teams to scalable products requires cross-organizational collaboration from the earliest stages, which is historically difficult in media companies
Mapping individual needs: AI leadership has been too poor at mapping out the specific needs of individual people and teams rather than pushing top-down solutions
Same news video content sometimes produced in five different places within BBC News without synchronization, causing inefficiency that AI reformatting aims to address

quotes.text: I don't work with AI. I work with news journalism and public service journalism in particular, and now of course we navigate in an emerging AI ecosystem.
quotes.speaker: Olle Zacharison
quotes.topic: Framing AI work as journalism-first, not technology-first

quotes.text: People don't come to the BBC News app or the website to get AI, right? You go there to get credible news... For us it's about how can we give people a better news experience, not an AI experience.
quotes.speaker: Olle Zacharison
quotes.topic: User-centric approach to AI in public service media

quotes.text: Just because it's an idea doesn't mean it's a good idea. Kill this idea in its sleep, basically.
quotes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

quotes.text: We've been in fifteen, twenty years of digital transformation, we've been obsessed with building things... Whereas now some of the stuff that we can do with Gen AI is like basically you can go in and in three hours customize the GPT that can perform a task that you had to build a tool for and using in six months.
quotes.speaker: Olle Zacharison
quotes.topic: Speed of GenAI prototyping vs. traditional tool-building

quotes.text: 1,224 ideas came in through that free text field, and some of those ideas were so brilliant. They were so brilliant that we could never think them up in a kind of strategy team because people know their workflows, right? They know the pain points of their everyday work.
quotes.speaker: Olle Zacharison
quotes.topic: Bottom-up innovation from staff who know their own pain points

quotes.text: Media industry executives are more easy to disrupt than news audiences, which is a very good thing.
quotes.speaker: Olle Zacharison
quotes.topic: Audience loyalty vs. executive anxiety about AI disruption

recommendations.advice: Form alliances with AI pioneers across the organization and maintain genuine curiosity about what other AI spearheads are thinking and working on; inspiration is the fuel of progress
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison
recommendations.target_audience: newsroom AI leaders seeking to move from experiments to organization-wide adoption

recommendations.advice: Start discovery with product, tech, and editorial teams together from the earliest stages of an idea rather than having the newsroom develop concepts in isolation and then handing off specifications
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison
recommendations.target_audience: media organizations struggling with cross-functional AI collaboration

recommendations.advice: Set very clear metrics and objectives for each AI project, and find the quickest possible way to pilot it; if you don't have clear metrics and an idea of what value you want to create, the project is dead in its sleep
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison

recommendations.advice: Aggregate bottom-up ideas from staff rather than relying solely on top-down strategy; use free-text surveys and open formats to surface workflow pain points that strategy teams would never identify on their own
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison

recommendations.advice: Organize AI work into strategic themes (e.g., productivity, reformatting, augmenting journalism, user experience) to connect standalone projects, coordinate across departments, and form long-term internal partnerships
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison
recommendations.target_audience: large news organizations managing multiple AI initiatives

recommendations.advice: Prioritize peer-to-peer learning between journalists over top-down AI evangelism; hearing from a colleague who uses AI in their daily work is more convincing than hearing from the head of AI
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison

recommendations.advice: Use product-side templates and processes for AI planning rather than editorial-style word documents; adopt the structured methodologies already used by tech and product teams
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison
recommendations.target_audience: editorial leaders collaborating with product and engineering teams

recommendations.advice: Lean into unique assets -- brand trust, direct audience traffic, curated archives, analog broadcast infrastructure -- rather than trying to emulate what AI assistants do
recommendations.speaker: Olle Zacharison
recommendations.target_audience: public broadcasters and legacy media companies
